15 Sousa W 1 88.000000 88.0 0.000000
18 Unspecified Vinho Verde W 219 87.041096 87.0 0.242009
16 Unspecified Vinho Verde R 65 86.061538 85.0 0.092308
17 Unspecified Vinho Verde Unspecified 22 83.590909 83.5 0.000000

Interpretation¶

The regional wine-type analysis reveals meaningful differences in review performance across both geography and wine style.

Several subregions demonstrate stronger performance among red wines than white wines, particularly when evaluating average review scores and the proportion of wines receiving scores of 90 points or higher.

Sousa and Monção e Melgaço exhibit particularly strong results among red wines, achieving some of the highest average scores and high-quality review rates observed in the dataset.

White wines remain well represented throughout the region, although review performance varies more substantially across subregions. These differences suggest that regional characteristics may interact with wine style, influencing how wines are perceived and evaluated by reviewers.

While sample size limitations should be considered when interpreting smaller groups, the findings provide evidence that both geography and wine type contribute to observed quality outcomes.

Visualizing Subregional Quality Profiles¶

Summary tables provide detailed statistics, but visualizations make it easier to compare multiple performance indicators simultaneously.

The following bubble chart combines three important measures of regional performance:

  • Average review score
  • Number of reviewed wines
  • Percentage of wines receiving scores of 90 points or higher

Bubble size represents review volume, while color intensity reflects the proportion of highly rated wines within each subregion. A vertical reference line indicates the overall average review score across all Vinho Verde wines included in the analysis.

This visualization provides a comprehensive view of how subregions compare in terms of quality, consistency, and review volume.

In [33]:
# Bubble plot: quality, volume, and high-quality rate by subregion
plot_data = subregion_data.groupby("subregion").agg(
    count=("regional_score", "count"),
    avg_score=("regional_score", "mean"),
    median_score=("regional_score", "median"),
    high_quality_rate=("high_quality_review", "mean")
).reset_index()

plot_data = plot_data.sort_values(by="avg_score", ascending=True)
overall_avg_score = subregion_data["regional_score"].mean()

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))

sns.scatterplot(
    data=plot_data,
    x="avg_score",
    y="subregion",
    size="count",
    hue="high_quality_rate",
    sizes=(100, 1200),
    palette="RdYlGn",
    alpha=0.75,
    edgecolor="black"
)

plt.axvline(
    overall_avg_score,
    color="black",
    linestyle="--",
    linewidth=1,
    label=f"Overall Avg: {overall_avg_score:.2f}"
)

plt.title("Vinho Verde Subregion Quality Profile")
plt.xlabel("Average Review Score")
plt.ylabel("Subregion")

plt.legend(
    bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1),
    loc="upper left",
    title="Review Count / 90+ Rate"
)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Interpretation¶

The bubble chart reveals several important regional patterns.

Sousa stands out as the highest-performing subregion based on average review score, exceeding the overall regional average and exhibiting one of the strongest concentrations of highly rated wines. Monção e Melgaço and Basto also demonstrate strong performance, combining above-average review scores with relatively high proportions of wines receiving 90 points or higher.

Ave and Lima represent larger portions of the reviewed wine population, contributing substantial review volume while maintaining average scores near or above the regional benchmark. Their larger bubble sizes indicate greater representation within the dataset, providing additional confidence in the stability of their average scores.

The chart also highlights the importance of considering review volume alongside average scores.

Smaller subregions may achieve exceptional average ratings but are based on fewer observations, while larger subregions provide more statistically stable performance indicators.

Overall, the analysis suggests that quality within Vinho Verde is not evenly distributed across the region. Certain subregions consistently outperform the regional average, indicating that geographic factors may play a meaningful role in wine quality outcomes.

Key Takeaway¶

The strongest-performing Vinho Verde subregions combine three characteristics:

  • Above-average review scores
  • Meaningful review volume
  • Higher concentrations of 90+ point wines

Sousa, Monção e Melgaço, and Basto emerge as particularly strong performers when these factors are considered together.

Regional Performance by Wine Type¶

The previous analysis evaluated quality performance at the subregional level. However, aggregate regional results may conceal important differences between red and white wines.

To explore this possibility, review performance was segmented by both subregion and wine type. The analysis focuses on the proportion of wines receiving scores of 90 points or higher, a commonly used benchmark for high-quality wines.

In the visualization below:

  • The x-axis represents the share of wines receiving scores of 90 points or higher.
  • Bubble size represents review volume.
  • Color distinguishes red and white wines.
  • The vertical reference line indicates the overall regional high-quality review rate.

This view helps identify whether specific subregions appear to outperform the regional benchmark for particular wine styles.

In [34]:
# Bubble plot: high-quality review rate by subregion and inferred wine type
wine_type_bubble = regional_wine_type_summary[
    regional_wine_type_summary["wine_type"].isin(["R", "W"])
].copy()

wine_type_bubble["wine_type_label"] = wine_type_bubble["wine_type"].map({
    "R": "Red",
    "W": "White"
})

overall_high_quality_rate = subregion_data["high_quality_review"].mean()

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))

sns.scatterplot(
    data=wine_type_bubble,
    x="high_quality_rate",
    y="subregion",
    hue="wine_type_label",
    size="wine_count",
    sizes=(100, 1200),
    palette={"Red": "#b2182b", "White": "#2166ac"},
    alpha=0.75,
    edgecolor="black"
)

plt.axvline(
    overall_high_quality_rate,
    color="black",
    linestyle="--",
    linewidth=1,
    label=f"Overall 90+ Rate: {overall_high_quality_rate:.2%}"
)

plt.title("High-Quality Review Rate by Subregion and Wine Type")
plt.xlabel("Share of Reviews Scored 90+")
plt.ylabel("Subregion")
plt.xlim(0, 1)

plt.legend(
    bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1),
    loc="upper left",
    title="Wine Type / Review Count"
)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Interpretation¶

The relationship between wine type and regional performance becomes more apparent when examining high-quality review rates.

Several subregions demonstrate stronger performance among red wines than white wines. Red wines from Sousa and Monção e Melgaço exhibit particularly high concentrations of 90-point-or-higher reviews, substantially exceeding the overall regional benchmark. These findings suggest that certain subregions may possess environmental or production characteristics that are especially favorable for high-performing red wines.

White wines generally exhibit lower high-quality review rates across many subregions, although they often account for a larger share of the reviewed wine population. This pattern indicates that while white wines remain an important component of Vinho Verde production, exceptional review performance may be concentrated within specific subregion-style combinations.

The visualization also highlights the importance of considering review volume alongside quality outcomes. Some subregions demonstrate exceptional review rates but are represented by relatively few wines, while others combine strong performance with larger review populations, providing greater confidence in the observed results.

Overall, the findings suggest that both geography and wine style influence quality outcomes, reinforcing the conclusion that wine quality is shaped by multiple interacting factors beyond chemical composition alone.

Key Takeaway¶

The strongest quality outcomes are not distributed evenly across the region. Certain subregion and wine-type combinations consistently outperform the overall Vinho Verde benchmark, suggesting that regional specialization may play an important role in producing highly rated wines.

Geographic Integration of Regional Results¶

Linking Quality Outcomes to Geographic Location¶

The previous analyses identified meaningful differences in review performance across Vinho Verde subregions. To better understand how these results are distributed geographically, the next stage of the analysis introduces approximate geographic coordinates for each recognized Vinho Verde subregion.

Because the review dataset does not include spatial boundary files, representative centroid coordinates were assigned to each subregion for visualization purposes. These coordinates are intended to provide geographic context rather than precise vineyard or municipal locations.

The objective of this section is to combine regional review performance with geographic information in preparation for map-based visualizations.

In [35]:
# Approximate centroid coordinates for Vinho Verde subregions.
# These are used for visualization only, not precise GIS boundary analysis.
subregion_locations = pd.DataFrame({
    "subregion": [
        "Moncao e Melgaco",
        "Lima",
        "Cavado",
        "Ave",
        "Basto",
        "Amarante",
        "Baiao",
        "Paiva",
        "Sousa"
    ],
    "latitude": [
        42.078, 41.767, 41.545, 41.380, 41.535,
        41.269, 41.163, 41.060, 41.200
    ],
    "longitude": [
        -8.478, -8.704, -8.426, -8.300, -7.875,
        -8.082, -8.035, -8.300, -8.350
    ]
})

subregion_locations
Out[35]:
subregion latitude longitude
0 Moncao e Melgaco 42.078 -8.478
1 Lima 41.767 -8.704
2 Cavado 41.545 -8.426
3 Ave 41.380 -8.300
4 Basto 41.535 -7.875
5 Amarante 41.269 -8.082
6 Baiao 41.163 -8.035
7 Paiva 41.060 -8.300
8 Sousa 41.200 -8.350

Geographic Dataset Construction¶

To visualize quality outcomes across the Vinho Verde region, the regional review statistics were merged with approximate geographic coordinates for each identified subregion.

This integration creates a geographic analysis dataset that combines review performance metrics with spatial location information. The resulting dataset allows quality outcomes to be visualized geographically while preserving the review-based measures developed throughout the regional analysis.

The dataset includes:

  • Subregion name
  • Review count
  • Average review score
  • Median review score
  • Maximum review score
  • Approximate geographic coordinates

This serves as the foundation for all subsequent map-based visualizations.

In [36]:
# Combine regional review outcomes with map coordinates
map_data = subregion_quality.reset_index().merge(
    subregion_locations,
    on="subregion",
    how="left"
)

map_data = map_data.dropna(subset=["latitude", "longitude"]).copy()
map_data
Out[36]:
subregion count avg_score median_score max_score latitude longitude
0 Sousa 23 89.913043 90.0 94 41.200 -8.350
1 Moncao e Melgaco 40 88.225000 88.0 92 42.078 -8.478
2 Basto 39 88.179487 88.0 93 41.535 -7.875
3 Ave 455 87.971429 88.0 96 41.380 -8.300
4 Lima 182 87.521978 88.0 95 41.767 -8.704
6 Amarante 2 86.500000 86.5 87 41.269 -8.082

Interpretation¶

The merged dataset successfully combines review performance and geographic location information for the major Vinho Verde subregions.

Several high-performing subregions identified earlier—including Sousa, Monção e Melgaço, and Basto—are now positioned geographically, allowing quality outcomes to be evaluated within a spatial context rather than solely through tables and charts.

This integration represents an important transition from statistical analysis to geographic visualization.

Incorporating Wine-Type Performance Metrics¶

The previous regional analyses demonstrated that quality outcomes can differ substantially between red and white wines. To preserve these findings within the geographic dataset, wine-type performance measures were added to each mapped subregion.

This enhancement allows future visualizations to examine not only overall subregional performance but also differences in review outcomes between red and white wines across the Vinho Verde region.

By combining geographic, quality, and wine-style information into a single dataset, the analysis provides a more comprehensive view of regional wine performance.

In [38]:
# Add red/white regional outcome detail to each mapped subregion
regional_pivot = regional_wine_type_summary.pivot_table(
    index="subregion",
    columns="wine_type",
    values=["avg_score", "high_quality_rate", "wine_count"],
    aggfunc="first"
)

regional_pivot.columns = [
    f"{metric}_{wine_type}" for metric, wine_type in regional_pivot.columns
]
regional_pivot = regional_pivot.reset_index()

map_data = map_data.merge(regional_pivot, on="subregion", how="left")
map_data.head()
Out[38]:
subregion count avg_score median_score max_score latitude longitude avg_score_R_x avg_score_Unspecified_x avg_score_W_x ... wine_count_W_x avg_score_R_y avg_score_Unspecified_y avg_score_W_y high_quality_rate_R_y high_quality_rate_Unspecified_y high_quality_rate_W_y wine_count_R_y wine_count_Unspecified_y wine_count_W_y
0 Sousa 23 89.913043 90.0 94 41.200 -8.350 90.294118 89.000000 88.000000 ... 1.0 90.294118 89.000000 88.000000 0.588235 0.400000 0.000000 17.0 5.0 1.0
1 Moncao e Melgaco 40 88.225000 88.0 92 42.078 -8.478 89.571429 85.500000 88.096774 ... 31.0 89.571429 85.500000 88.096774 0.714286 0.000000 0.354839 7.0 2.0 31.0
2 Basto 39 88.179487 88.0 93 41.535 -7.875 88.730769 88.500000 84.800000 ... 5.0 88.730769 88.500000 84.800000 0.538462 0.250000 0.000000 26.0 8.0 5.0
3 Ave 455 87.971429 88.0 96 41.380 -8.300 88.250000 88.152941 87.088235 ... 102.0 88.250000 88.152941 87.088235 0.309701 0.423529 0.205882 268.0 85.0 102.0
4 Lima 182 87.521978 88.0 95 41.767 -8.704 87.788136 87.869565 86.560976 ... 41.0 87.788136 87.869565 86.560976 0.254237 0.434783 0.170732 118.0 23.0 41.0

5 rows × 25 columns

Interpretation¶

The enriched geographic dataset now contains both overall review performance and wine-type-specific outcomes for each mapped subregion.

This integrated structure supports more detailed geographic exploration by allowing regional review performance to be examined alongside differences between red and white wines.

The resulting dataset serves as a bridge between the physicochemical modeling performed in Phase 1 and the geographic review analysis conducted in Phase 2.

Regional Quality Map¶

Visualizing Quality Across the Vinho Verde Region¶

The previous analyses identified meaningful differences in review performance across Vinho Verde subregions. While tables and bubble charts quantify these differences, geographic visualization provides additional context by showing where these performance patterns occur within the region.

The map below plots each subregion using approximate geographic coordinates. Marker size represents review volume, while marker color reflects average review score.

This visualization allows regional performance to be evaluated spatially and highlights areas where highly rated wines appear to be concentrated.

In [39]:
# Static map-style scatter plot for notebook/report output
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))

scatter = plt.scatter(
    map_data["longitude"],
    map_data["latitude"],
    s=map_data["count"] * 25,
    c=map_data["avg_score"],
    cmap="viridis",
    alpha=0.75,
    edgecolor="black"
)

for _, row in map_data.iterrows():
    plt.text(
        row["longitude"] + 0.015,
        row["latitude"] + 0.015,
        row["subregion"],
        fontsize=9
    )

plt.colorbar(scatter, label="Average Review Score")
plt.title("Vinho Verde Subregion Review Quality Map")
plt.xlabel("Longitude")
plt.ylabel("Latitude")
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Interpretation¶

The regional quality map reinforces several findings observed throughout the statistical analysis.

Sousa, Monção e Melgaço, and Basto remain among the strongest-performing subregions based on average review scores. Their geographic locations illustrate that high-performing areas are distributed across multiple parts of the Vinho Verde region rather than concentrated in a single cluster.

Larger subregions such as Ave and Lima contribute substantially more reviewed wines while maintaining competitive review scores, indicating that quality performance is not limited to smaller specialty areas.

Although the map is intended for visualization rather than detailed geographic modeling, it provides valuable context for understanding how review performance varies throughout the Vinho Verde region.

Combined with the physicochemical analysis from Phase 1, these findings suggest that wine quality may be influenced by both measurable chemical characteristics and regional factors.

Interactive Regional Quality Explorer¶

Integrating Quality, Geography, and Wine Type¶

The previous visualizations established that wine quality varies across Vinho Verde subregions and that performance patterns differ between red and white wines. While static charts provide useful comparisons, interactive mapping allows these regional relationships to be explored in greater detail.

The interactive map combines multiple dimensions of the analysis into a single visualization:

  • Geographic location of each subregion
  • Review volume
  • Average review score
  • Median and maximum review scores
  • Red wine performance metrics
  • White wine performance metrics
  • Proportion of wines receiving scores of 90 points or higher

Marker size represents review volume, while marker color reflects average review score. Selecting a marker reveals detailed performance information for each subregion.

This visualization serves as the final integration point between the physicochemical quality analysis performed in Phase 1 and the regional review analysis conducted in Phase 2.

How to Read the Map¶

This interactive map visualizes regional review performance across the Vinho Verde region.

  • Circle color represents average review score.
  • Circle size represents the number of reviewed wines.
  • Popup windows provide additional review statistics, including red and white wine performance where available.

The color scale ranges from lower average review scores to higher average review scores, while larger circles indicate greater review volume. Combining these measures helps distinguish meaningful regional performance patterns from results based on only a small number of reviews.

In [40]:
import folium
import branca.colormap as cm

def format_score(value):
    if pd.notnull(value):
        return f"{value:.2f}"
    return "N/A"

def format_rate(value):
    if pd.notnull(value):
        return f"{value:.2%}"
    return "N/A"

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import folium
import branca.colormap as cm

# Color scale based on average review score
min_score = map_data["avg_score"].min()
max_score = map_data["avg_score"].max()

color_scale = cm.LinearColormap(
    colors=["#d73027", "#fee08b", "#1a9850"],
    vmin=min_score,
    vmax=max_score,
    caption="Average Review Score"
)

vinho_map = folium.Map(
    location=[41.45, -8.25],
    zoom_start=9,
    tiles="OpenStreetMap"
)

for _, row in map_data.iterrows():
    red_avg = row.get("avg_score_R", np.nan)
    white_avg = row.get("avg_score_W", np.nan)
    red_hq = row.get("high_quality_rate_R", np.nan)
    white_hq = row.get("high_quality_rate_W", np.nan)

    marker_color = color_scale(row["avg_score"])

    popup_text = f"""
    <b>{row['subregion']}</b><br>
    Review count: {row['count']:.0f}<br>
    Avg review score: {row['avg_score']:.2f}<br>
    Median review score: {row['median_score']:.2f}<br>
    Max review score: {row['max_score']:.0f}<br><br>
    Red avg score: {format_score(red_avg)}<br>
    White avg score: {format_score(white_avg)}<br>
    Red 90+ rate: {format_rate(red_hq)}<br>
    White 90+ rate: {format_rate(white_hq)}
    """

    folium.CircleMarker(
        location=[row["latitude"], row["longitude"]],
        radius=max(6, min(24, row["count"] * 2)),
        popup=folium.Popup(popup_text, max_width=350),
        tooltip=f"{row['subregion']}: {row['avg_score']:.2f}",
        color="black",
        weight=1,
        fill=True,
        fill_color=marker_color,
        fill_opacity=0.75
    ).add_to(vinho_map)

color_scale.add_to(vinho_map)

vinho_map
Out[40]:
Make this Notebook Trusted to load map: File -> Trust Notebook

Map Interpretation¶

The regional review data adds geographic context to the red-versus-white quality models, but it should not be interpreted as the same dataset. The UCI physicochemical dataset identifies measurable chemical characteristics associated with wine quality, while the regional review dataset identifies subregions where highly rated Vinho Verde wines appear in published reviews.

When interpreting the regional results, greater emphasis should be placed on subregions with meaningful review counts. A subregion with a high average score but only one or two reviewed wines should be viewed as directional rather than conclusive.

Together, these analyses support a broader conclusion: wine quality in Vinho Verde can be examined through both measurable wine chemistry and external market-review signals. However, a true vineyard- or subregion-level predictive model would require a unified dataset containing physicochemical measurements, wine type, quality score, and precise subregional information for the same wines.

Strategic Insight¶

This analysis demonstrates that wine quality can be evaluated through two complementary dimensions. The first focuses on measurable physicochemical characteristics that influence wine structure and sensory perception.

The second examines how external market validation is distributed across geographic regions.

The strongest understanding of wine quality emerges when these perspectives are considered together, highlighting both the science of wine production and the regional factors associated with market recognition.

Key Findings¶

Finding 1: Alcohol Was The Most Consistent Quality Indicator¶

Across exploratory analysis, correlation analysis, and predictive modeling, alcohol content consistently emerged as the strongest positive predictor of wine quality.

Finding 2: Quality Drivers Differ By Wine Type¶

Red and white wines exhibited different quality drivers, suggesting that wine quality cannot be explained using a universal model and that style-specific production characteristics play an important role in quality outcomes.

Finding 3: Geography Appears To Influence Review Outcomes¶

Several Vinho Verde subregions consistently outperformed regional averages, indicating that geographic factors may contribute to perceived wine quality.

Finding 4: Chemistry And Geography Provide Complementary Perspectives¶

Neither dataset independently explains wine quality. Together they provide a broader framework for understanding wine performance.

Discussion of Results¶

One of the most notable findings from this analysis was not the importance of any single variable, but rather the consistency with which different analytical approaches pointed toward the same conclusion. Physicochemical analysis identified measurable characteristics associated with wine quality, while regional review analysis highlighted geographic concentrations of highly rated wines.

Importantly, the study demonstrates that confidence in analytical findings increases when independent methods arrive at similar conclusions. Across exploratory analysis, correlation analysis, logistic regression, and Random Forest modeling, several key variables repeatedly emerged as important indicators of wine quality. This convergence strengthens trust in the findings and highlights the value of using multiple analytical perspectives when evaluating complex systems.

These findings suggest that wine quality is best understood through independent analytical approaches rather than any single measurement or model.

Practical Implications¶

For Winemakers¶

The findings suggest that managing alcohol content, volatile acidity, sulfur-related characteristics, and style-specific production variables may influence the likelihood of producing higher-quality wines.

For Consumers¶

Regional review patterns may help identify Vinho Verde subregions that consistently produce highly rated wines.

For Researchers¶

The inability to directly connect chemistry and geography represents the most significant opportunity for future study. Integrating these dimensions would enable more comprehensive predictive models of wine quality.

Conclusions¶

This project examined wine quality through two complementary perspectives: physicochemical characteristics and regional review performance.

The first phase leveraged the UCI Vinho Verde Wine Quality dataset to identify measurable characteristics associated with high-quality wines. Across exploratory analysis, correlation analysis, logistic regression modeling, and Random Forest validation, several physicochemical characteristics consistently emerged as meaningful indicators of wine quality, with alcohol content demonstrating the most stable relationship across analytical methods.

These findings suggest that wine quality is not driven by a single factor, but rather by a combination of interacting physicochemical properties that differ across wine styles.

The second phase expanded the analysis beyond laboratory measurements by incorporating a regional wine-review dataset containing geographic information from the Vinho Verde region. This analysis revealed meaningful variation in review performance across subregions and demonstrated that highly rated wines are not distributed evenly throughout the region.

Taken together, the findings suggest that wine quality is best understood through the interaction of measurable physicochemical characteristics and regional influences. While laboratory measurements provide insight into the structural properties associated with quality, regional review patterns suggest that geographic factors may also contribute to how wine quality is expressed and recognized.

Neither perspective alone fully explains wine quality. However, when considered together, they provide a more comprehensive framework for understanding why certain wines consistently outperform others.

Beyond wine quality, this project demonstrates the value of integrating exploratory analysis, predictive modeling, validation techniques, and geographic investigation to evaluate complex questions from integrated sources of evidence. The strongest conclusions did not emerge from any single model or visualization, but from the consistency of findings across independent analytical approaches.

This reflects a broader principle of data analytics: meaningful understanding is often achieved through the integration of complementary sources of evidence. By combining multiple analytical methods and perspectives, organizations can develop greater confidence in their findings and make more informed decisions in complex environments.

Limitations¶

Several limitations should be considered when interpreting the results.

The physicochemical dataset does not contain vineyard, producer, municipality, or subregional information, preventing direct geographic analysis. Likewise, the regional review dataset does not contain laboratory measurements for the reviewed wines.

As a result, the relationship between chemistry and geography cannot be established directly within this project. The conclusions should therefore be interpreted as complementary analytical perspectives rather than a single integrated predictive model.

Additionally, some Vinho Verde subregions contained relatively few reviews, making their results more sensitive to individual observations than larger subregions.

Future Research¶

The greatest opportunity identified through this analysis is the development of an integrated wine intelligence dataset that combines laboratory measurements, wine type, producer information, quality ratings, and geographic origin for the same wines.

The current project examined wine quality through two complementary but independent perspectives: physicochemical characteristics and regional review performance. While each dataset provided valuable insights, the inability to directly connect chemistry, geography, and market outcomes represents the most significant analytical gap identified during this study.

An integrated dataset would enable researchers to move beyond identifying quality indicators and begin evaluating how production characteristics, regional influences, and market perception interact to shape wine quality outcomes.

Future research could support:¶

  • Direct modeling of regional quality drivers
  • Comparison of physicochemical profiles across subregions
  • More precise prediction of high-quality wines
  • Evaluation of geographic influences on wine quality
  • Development of vineyard- and producer-level predictive models
  • Assessment of environmental and terroir-related factors associated with highly rated wines

More broadly, such a framework would allow wine quality to be examined as a connected system rather than as separate chemical, geographic, and review-based observations. By integrating these dimensions into a unified analytical model, future studies could provide a more comprehensive understanding of why certain wines consistently outperform others and how those outcomes can be predicted more effectively.

In [ ]:
